skill.name: py-sqlmodel-patterns
skill.description: Guides SQLModel and async SQLAlchemy work with patterns for models, relationships, queries, UUIDs, sessions, and ORM debugging.
skill.summary: Apply practical SQLModel and async SQLAlchemy patterns for reliable database code.
skill.author: CJHarmath

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, environment, weak crypto, reconnaissance, and combined-risk patterns. Review found these are documentation examples, reference links, and local grep suggestions inside SKILL.md, with no automatic execution, exfiltration, prompt injection, or malicious intent. The skill is safe to publish with low risk because it includes optional local shell commands for code search.

low_findings.title: Static command findings are documentation examples
low_findings.description: The flagged Python blocks demonstrate SQLModel and SQLAlchemy usage. They are fenced Markdown examples and are not executed by the skill. This dismisses the Ruby backtick and command execution findings for the instructional code blocks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is entirely inside Markdown code fences that teach ORM patterns. No file in scope contains executable Ruby, shell backticks, or a launcher that runs these examples.

low_findings.title: Local grep commands are optional code search guidance
low_findings.description: The bash commands search local Python files for ORM patterns. They do not use network access, credentials, file modification, or dynamic command construction. This is a low-risk external command pattern because users may choose to run it manually.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible grep pipelines with fixed patterns. They are not auto-executed by the skill, but they are still shell commands, so a low-risk classification is appropriate.

low_findings.title: Reference URLs are not runtime network access
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to SQLModel and SQLAlchemy documentation. They are plain Markdown references and do not send data or credentials to external services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in a References section and are not part of executable code. No evidence found of HTTP clients, webhooks, or data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Database URL appears only in a configuration example
low_findings.description: DATABASE_URL is used as an example argument for create_async_engine. The skill does not read environment variables or transmit connection strings.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The variable is shown in a fenced Python example for SQL echo logging. No evidence found of secret access outside the illustrative snippet.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography and reconnaissance matches are false positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines contain ordinary words, ORM fields, UUID examples, and query snippets. They do not contain weak cryptographic algorithms, system reconnaissance commands, or host inventory logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no crypto API calls or reconnaissance utilities at these locations. The matches appear to be token-level false positives from database and documentation text.

content.value_statement: Async ORM code often fails through lazy loading, unclear schemas, and session state mistakes. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical SQLModel patterns for safer database work.
actual_capabilities: Explains eager loading choices for async SQLAlchemy relationships.
Shows how to separate SQLModel table models from API schemas.
Guides session handling with commit, refresh, and expire_on_commit behavior.
Covers UUID fields, nullable fields, relationships, and query patterns.
Provides PostgreSQL upsert guidance for insert-or-update workflows.
Suggests local grep checks for lazy loading and missing relationship options.
limitations: It is a written pattern guide, not an executable database tool.
It focuses on SQLModel with async SQLAlchemy and PostgreSQL examples.
It does not inspect a live database schema or migration history.
It does not replace application-specific performance testing.

use_cases.title: Fix async relationship errors
use_cases.description: Diagnose lazy loading failures and choose selectinload or joinedload for the relationship shape.

use_cases.title: Design clean API schemas
use_cases.description: Separate table models from create, read, and update schemas to avoid leaking sensitive fields.

use_cases.title: Review database query patterns
use_cases.description: Check pagination, counts, existence checks, and upserts for common SQLModel mistakes.

prompt_templates.title: Review a SQLModel model
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of model and schema structure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my SQLModel model and API schemas. Check field defaults, sensitive fields, relationships, and whether the create, read, and update schemas are separated correctly.

prompt_templates.title: Debug async ORM loading
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate debugging of relationship access errors.
prompt_templates.prompt: I am seeing async SQLAlchemy relationship errors. Review this query and explain whether I should use selectinload or joinedload for each relationship.

prompt_templates.title: Improve query performance
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review for N+1 queries and pagination.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit these SQLModel queries for N+1 risks, missing eager loading, incorrect scalar usage, and pagination issues. Suggest concise changes.

prompt_templates.title: Design an upsert workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a PostgreSQL upsert workflow for this SQLModel table. Include conflict keys, updated fields, session handling, and validation concerns.

output_examples.input: Review a query that loads users and then reads each user's team.
output_examples.output: The result should identify an N+1 risk, recommend eager loading for the team relationship, and explain why the async session should not rely on lazy loading.

output_examples.input: Check a SQLModel user table that includes email, hashed password, and created time.
output_examples.output: The result should recommend separate create, read, and update schemas, and it should keep password fields out of public response schemas.

output_examples.input: Plan an insert-or-update operation for user answers.
output_examples.output: The result should describe the conflict key, values to update, commit behavior, and when to refresh returned objects.

best_practices: Use eager loading for relationships that will be read in async code.
Keep database table models separate from public API response schemas.
Refresh objects after commit when database-generated values are needed.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on lazy relationship access in async SQLAlchemy code.
Do not expose hashed passwords or plain passwords in read schemas.
Do not compare UUID database columns to unchecked string values.

faq.question: Does this skill run database commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples for SQLModel and async SQLAlchemy code.

faq.question: Can it help with greenlet_spawn errors?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains why lazy loading fails in async code and how eager loading helps.

faq.question: Does it support synchronous SQLAlchemy?
faq.answer: The examples focus on async SQLAlchemy, but some modeling guidance also applies to synchronous code.

faq.question: Can it design FastAPI request and response schemas?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers create, read, and update schema separation for SQLModel applications.

faq.question: Does it inspect my project automatically?
faq.answer: No. It can guide Claude, Codex, or Claude Code while they review files you provide.

faq.question: Is it tied to PostgreSQL?
faq.answer: Most patterns are general, but the upsert example uses PostgreSQL-specific behavior.
